CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Most, of our readers will have read a détailed account
of the Language Procession in the daily Press, so that we
need not refer at length to its details. ~ It was the largest
Language Procession that ever paraded the streets of

‘Dublin. The two leading notes were the great increase
in the industrial section, and the reply to the anti-Trish
bigots of the Post Office. The Post Office in, though not
of, Ireland over which a Mr. Egerton, an imported Bri-

 tisher, presides was, we were glad to sce, treated to a
special tableau.’ The skirmishes that our readers en-
gaged.in at Riverstown, Carlow, and Gorey have now
developed, and ‘there is open war between the anti-Irish
Post Office and the whole force of Irish Ireland.  One
does not need the gift of prophesy to sce that the anti-
Irish Post Office must éventually surrender. The indus-
trial section showed a great advance on that of last year.
Amongst, the industrial exhibits were those from Messrs.
Perry and Co., box makers ; Messrs, Kapp and Peterson,
pipe makers; Irish dolls made by Messrs. O’Moore and
Co., Ltd.. Capel Street; boxes made by the firm of
O'Reilly, Poolbeg Street; blacking, metal polish, ete.,

by Messrs. Malone, Bros. (late M‘Menamin and Malone) ;
E nd Son, hop bitter manufacturers ; Pater-
Murphy’s starch; M‘Menamin Bros.

#

s

blacking, etc. ; Lucania bicycles, by John O'Neill, South
King Street; a splendid exhibition of fibrous plaster-
work by John Ryan, of Upper Abbey Street ; an elaborate
exhibit, from the Dolphin’s Barn Brick Works; brushes
by Varian and Co. ; exhibits from the Limerick Shirt
Factory, and hosiery frém the Blackrock Hosiery
Company; Smyth and Co. rain proof, etc.; Loughlin,
Parliament Street, poplin ties, etc.; Duffy and Son,
publishers; Messrs. Malone, sculptors; Messrs. Teanch
and Owen, tanners, etc.

His Grace the Archbishop attended at Smithfield and
spoke after Dr. Hyde’s addresses in Irish and English, at
the meeting. In the course of his address, his Grace
remarked : —“T had an opportunity of seeing your pro-
cession at one or two points as it pasesd through. the
streets of our city. I have no doubt that throughout
its course, from beginning to end, it was characterised
by the same good order, and, let me say, not only order,
but impressive dignity, that marked every step in the
proceedings here (applause), as the various parts of the
procession were passing into the positions assigned to
them in this great market-place (applause).” ' That
tribute was well deserved even though it will further on
be seen that there was an exception to the almost com-
plete spirit of good order and dignity which so favour-
ably impressed his Grace.  His Grace also said:—
“Within the last few years, thanks to the splencid
energy of the movement that has found expression 1n
the gathering of this vast assemblage, Dublin has be-
come the chief centre of one of the strongest of the forces
that are at work for the restoration of the life of our
Trish nation (prolonged applause)—that force which, in
spite of many formidable obstacles, is steadily. progress-
ing in the work of bringing back our national language
to its rightful place in the life of the people of Ireland
(renewed applause). Let us hope and pray that the
sphere within which that force has hitherto worked will
now rapidly widen.” We welcome his Grace’s active
participation in the movement, and we hope that it
will result in the active participation in it of thousands
of others, who ought to be in it, and whose influence in
directing and steadying it is much to be desired. To his
Grace’s remark, “Let us hope and pray that the sphere
within which that force has hitherto worked will now
rapidly widen,” we beg to articulate a hearty Amen.
The movement is all right; the League, though imper-
fect like all human institutions, is fairly all right. Toa
much centralization combined with a narrow, if not
spiteful, resentment of criticism at the headquarters
would lead—particularly if the critics were weak enough
to be intimidated off the field—in an unhealthy direc-
tion. We welcome his Grace’s active participation in
the movement, and we hope that his example will be
followed. If people of weight do not take their full
share in the movement, people lacking weight will
naturally have a disproportionate influence in it-—and
ultimately that would not lead to a healthy state of
affairs. 5

There was a regrettable and disgraceful, but happily
isolated, instance of Hooliganism at the Language Pro-'
cession on Sunday. The carriage in which Dr. M‘Wal-
ter, in his capacity as member of the Corporation of
Dublin, attended the procession was held up by roughs.
one of whom wore a badge showing that he was a steward
of the procession. Dr. M‘Walter, as a member of the
Corporation, was invited to the procession by the Gaelic
League, and it is for the authorities of the Gaelic League
to apologise for and repudiate—and that at once—the
blackguardism that was meted out to him in the streets
of Dublin by a gang of roughs, one wearing the badge of
steward.  The members of the Corporation broughtiup

the rere of the great procession, so that as Dr. M‘Wal-
ter’s carriage was almost at the very tail end it was com-
paratively easy for-the roughs to hold it up, and prevent
it proceeding along in the procession. Now the roughs
_were not attacking Dr. M‘Walter. Dr. M‘Walter in-
vited Mr, John Gore, the eminent solicitor and temper-
ance advocate, to accompany him in the carriage as his
guest, if we may use the word in this connection. So far,
so good. Dr. M‘Walter and Mr. John Gore would ‘luwc
gone through all right, but Dr. M‘Walter also kindly
invited the editor of this paper to accompany him. The
editor of this paper accepted the kind invitation, and
his foul, nefarious personality was the cause of bringing
the roughs on the track of Dr. M‘Walter'’s carriage.
It is now for the authorities of the League to at once
apologise for and repudiate this street blackguardism,
for one with the badge of steward was among the
roughs.

The ringleader refused to give his name; we know the
fellow’s appearance, and if we saw him again would re-
cognise him. One of the Hooligans charged us that we
“slandered the procession!”  And the ringleader—the
coward who was afraid to give his name—warned the
editor that he (the editor) would be responsible for any
consequences if we objected to be held up by these
roughs. So that if we whipped on against these cowards
they would hold us responsible if we hurt one of them!
Now, there were three ways of meeting these blackguards.
One was out of the question—and, no doubt, the cowards
knew it—it was to call the minions of the law—get the
police to scatter them. One D.M.P. would have driven
terror into the whole gang; the second was to whip over
them. That course, in view of the great crowds, might
have led to a crush and a panic amongst the people, and
if a few of the public were seriously hurt and killed as a
-consequence, it would be very regrettable, and the asser-
tion of a member of the Corporation’s right as a citizen
against the cowardly interference of the roughs under
the circumstances could scarcely be justified ; it certainly
- would not be prudent; anyway, people would naturally
not care to run such risks of injuring innocent people
merely for the sake of asserting Irish citizens’ rights
against a gang of cowards; the third course was to get
round off the line of procession, and join in later on.
The third course was attempted, but the Hooligans were
agile upon the feet, and a more or less similar situation
had to be faced more than once. It was a matter of
whipping over the Hooligans or calling the police. ~After
all, the wisest and most dignified cowrse for the member
of the Corporation, whose guests we were, was to give up
the idea of proceeding with the procession in which he
was officially invited by the Gaelic League to participate.
He accordingly drove to one of his houses in the city, and
s0 the matter ended; these Hooligans in the Language
Procession had gained their point.

For our part owr chief regret was that our foul ne-
farious selves, by being the guest of Dr. M‘Walter,
brought this annoyance upon him. The Hooligans
would have allowed Dr. McWalter and his friend Mr.
John Gore, Solicitor, {o pass through, if the obnoxious
editor of this paper, the third occupant of the carriage,
would rid the carriage of his presence at the behest of
these roughs. Of cowrse that was out of the question,
and so Dr. McWalter attending the procession as a
member of the Corporation of Dublin was stopped on
the route by roughs, one of whom wore the badge of a
Steward of the Language Procession. After all, the
tactics of footpads is not a reply to criticism, black-
guardism is not intellectual retort, and Hooliganism
is not argument. An interesting commentary upon the
whole proceedings will probably suggest itself to the
minds of most of our readers. If the LrapEr had not
fought the anti-Irish elements for over four years and
a half, what is the likelihood that, any language pro-
cimﬁr; would have been seen in the streets of Dublin
ap al

At Monday’s meeting of the Dublin Corporation, Dr.
McWalter very properly referred to the outrage of the

preceding day.  He raised the mattﬂcr as one invo]vfng
a breach of the privileges of the Council. According
to a newspaper report, he said : —"As members were
aware, the Corporation had been invited to take part in -
the Irish Language procession ycsbc_rduy Following
that invitation, the carriage of a certain member of the
Corporation was stopped, and he was told that he would
not be allowed to continue in the procession. That
appeared to him to be a grave breach of prlyl]egc, and
on any future occasion it would not be consistent with
the digniby of the Council to take part in any demon-
stration organised by the same parties unless an explana~
tion of the incident in question was given, and an
apology forthcoming. It was unnecessary to mention
the member’s name. There was not any objection to
him personally. The cause of the incident was that the
member had given a seat in his carriage to the editor
of a certain Dublin paper, and was told he would have
to make him leave the carriage, a request with which he
refused to comply. He (Dr. M‘Walter), believed that
the action was that of an irresponsible person, and the
necessary apology would be forthcoming.”

Many of our readers will be pleased to hear that it is
proposed to erect a memorial over the grave where
Miceat O Mamnin of Ballyferiter lies, for we know that
many of our readers knew him personally; and to know
him was to love him. A Committee has been formed
and circulars ave being distributed amongst the teachers,
branches of the Gaelic League, and to individuals to
whom the late Mr. Manning’s memory is dear. As the
circular before us says:—“Of Mr. Manning as a Gael,
a gentleman, and a true patriot, it is unnecessary to
speak. Even his natural modesty could not hide away
the splendid qualities with which he was so richly
endowed. Every Irish Irelander mourns his loss, and
is anxious both to honour the man who is gone, and
to preserve his spirit and example for the nation’s good.”
hose of our readers who, like ourselves, have spent
many pleasant, hours with the kindly Irishman of Bally-
ferriter, who dropped out of the ranks when his heart
was warm and aglow in the cause of Irish Ireland, will
have pleasure in helping on the work of erecting a
memorial to him. Mr. Thomas O’Donnell, M.P., and
Mr. M. O’Connor, Lispole, are Hon. Secretaries of the
Memorial Fund, and the treasurer, to whom subscrip-
tions may be sent, is the Very Rev. D. Canon O’Leary,
P.P., Dingle, Co. Kerry.

The testimonial to Police-Sergeant Gallagher of Lur-
gan has come off. It will be remembered that one
Andrew Donnelly of the Lurgan Commercial Hotel,
was made treasurer of the collection at first. Alas, for
Myr. Donnelly of the Commercial, the presentation took
place at the Grand. We read :—“A pleasant few hours
was spent in songs, recitations, and speeches, which
were given by Messrs. George Corry, T. Lynch, Austin
M‘Anally, M. M‘Entee, Hugh Ward, etc., and after
refreshments and supper, the proceedings terminated.”
And so one hotel man was treasurer at first, and yet
another who took up the job later had the serving of the
refreshments and supper. We hear that the purse only
contained about ten sovereigns! Mr. Donnelly of the
Commercial Hotel, the original treasurer, did not, we
hgar, appreciate the notoriety he got as a consequence of
his holding the great office, so he resigned, and a brother
Bung, a Mr. M, McEntee, took up the job with alacrity
and  the “refreshments and supper” came off at
McEntee’s “establishment.” The chairman of this
Peeler Testimonial, this testimonial to a foul minion
of the law, was one George Corry, R.D.C. President
of the Cornakinnegar bramch of the United Trish
League. Weo wonder how much change had poor Ser-

geant Gallagher oub of the purse when Bung was paid ?*

If a peeler in Luwrgan gets a purse of sovereigns,

there is a peeler in Banbridge who recently got a testi- -

menial of a different kind from some Orange ruffians.
It was on a recent Sunday evening that three orange
lambs were keeping the Sabbath. Their names were
Samuel Preston, Stewart Connolly and William J.
Noddings. Sergeant Wishart met the three Orange

lambs and, to quote from the report in the [rish News—
"Noddmgs‘ waved his arms and shouted, “To hell with
the Pope.” ‘Go on the Blues” Witness cautioned him
regarding such conduct, and Connolly who was: behind
Noddings, started yelling ‘To hell with the Pope,’ ‘No
swrrender.’ The three defendants then went round the
corner of Linenhall-Street, and went over to a pile of
stones ab the new buildings and commenced to throw
stones at witness. A crowd quickly gathered to the
spot. The defendants continued yelling and shouting
and dared witness to come on. Noddings came out on
S e to the middle of the road and shouted ‘Hell roast the
Pope,’” and Connolly yelled ‘IIell roast his b soul.’
They continued stoning and cwsing witness and
threatened him to arrest them. = Witness sent to the
barrack for assistance and advanced towards the defen-
dants. They ran off and the crowd cheered and fol-
lowed. At the workhouse gates the defendants halted
at a pile of stones aud started to stone him, again
cursing the Pope and witness all the time.” The Ser-
geant did not wish to press the charge against Preston,
who stated that he was an importation from Glasgow.
Preston, the Sergeant said, had not made use of any
party expressions, and endeavoured to get the others to
leave down the stones, but on the other two he would
like the Bench to inflict the extreme penalty of the
statute. The magistrates, who were Dr. William Smyth,
/ J.P., and Howard Ferguson, let Preston off and fined
{ the other ruffians 20s. and costs or fourteen days! If
ruffianism of this sort is dealt with in this easy manner
by Banbridge magistrates, we will not be surprised to
hear of several more Sababth affairs of this kind on
the part of “Saved” Banbridge Orange blackguards.

Atthefirst conference of delegates from Camozuroeascc

clubs, which was held recently at the Keating branch
rooms, it was decided to form a Central Council. It
was agreed there should be an Ard-Choiste of 8 elected
by the delegates preseut with a quarterly meeting of
delegates from all Camoguidheacht Clubs to which all
important decisions come to by the Ard-Choiste, should
be submitted for ratification. It was decided that the
Ard-Choiste should have the power of deciding in all
disputes, and that they should proceed to form a
Camoguidheacht League. The affiliation fee for Clubs
was fixed ab bs. yearly. The resolution proposed by the
Dundrum Volunteer Comoguidheacht Club that all
players should wear material of Irish manufacture, was
passed unanimously. The first quarterly meeting of
delegates was fixed provisionally for Saturday the 27th
May.

Bung waited on the British Chancellor of the Ex-
chequer a few days ago The deputation represented the
Bung interest of the three kingdoms, and one Francis
O'Neill, past chairman, and one E. Corcoran, Secretary,
represented the Belfast and Ulster Bungs Association.
Pat Martin, the new Emperor of the Dublin Bungs, was
also present and made a speech, but the report hefore
us is that which appeared in the Belfast Zveming
Telegraph, and it is chiefly composed of an account of
the remarks of Francis O'Neill. Now this particular
Belfast Bung, Francis O'Neill, is a_“tried and true”
Nationalist, a staunch United Irish Leaguer. Indeed,
the United Irish League in Belfast is, we hear, largely
maintained by Bung. Most of these patriotic Bungs have
their houses in “Saved” districts, so that they are subject
to a temptation to prove their tolerance. Well now,
this Mr. O'Neill, “patriot,” in the course of his remarks,
said :—“In_consequence of the decision of the Court of
Appeal in Ireland a few days ago, this new principle of
valuation will doubtless be applied to all other parts of
Ireland within a short time, and unless we obtain some
relief from these excessive burdens through the present
administration, whom we in Belfast and the province
of Ulster look to as friends, the extinction of those now
engaged in this important trade will be the inevitable
result. In conclusion, we earnestly hope that as we
have hitherto willingly borne our share, and, indeed,
more than our share, of the taxation, due to the
South African war, that the time has now come to re-
ceive consideration al your hands; and in view of the
fact that a considerable surplus is anticipated at the
end of the present financial year, we confidently ask
you to remit the extra duties imposed on spirits and
beer in 1890 and 1900.” And so this “patriotic” Bung,
this “tried and true” Nationalist, the staunch United
Irish Leaguer of Belfast, Francis O'Neill, goes over to
the British Chancellor of the Exchequer and pleads for
favours, and says that “we have hitherto willingly borne
our share, and, indeed, more than our share, of the
taxation due to the South African War.” We wonder
what does the United Irish League of Belfast think of
this “ hwro for Tommy Atkins and Lord Roberts”
staunch United Irish Leaguer, Francis O’Neill, Bung of
Belfast? But perhaps the other “tried and true” Bungs
of Belfast are not much betler.

‘We would like to remind our readers that after the
first Gospel at High Mass (12 o'clock), at the Pro-
Cathedral, Marlborough Street, a sermon in Irish will
be preached by Rev. Dr. O'Hickey, the distinguished
Trish Professor at Maynooth.

We hope there will be a great congregation at the . .../
Passionist Church, Mount Argus, on St. Patrick’s night.
This was the first Church in Dublin, we believe, in which
an Irish sermon was preached in modern times. The ser~
vice will commence at & o'clock with a Rosary in Irish.
This will be followed by the singing of the Irish version
of the Hymn to St. Patrick, arranged by Rev. George
O'Neill, 8.J. The sermon will be preached by Father
MacInerney, of St. Andrew’s, Westland Row, a well-
known Irish speaker and prominent figure in the Lan-
guage Movement. There will be nc English during the
entire ceremony ; the language throughout, so far as the
liturgy allows, will be Irish. We hope that there will be
a greab rally of Trish Ireland Catholics te Mount Avgus
on Friday night.

In the matterof an Trish sermon on St. Patrick’s Day
we are glad to hear that Cork will be in line with Irish
Treland this year During Pontifical High Mass (12
o'clock) on St. Patrick’s Day a sermon in Irish will be
preached in the Cathedral, Cork, by the Very Rev. M.
Canon Shinkwin, P.P., V.F., of Bantry. Canon Shink- .
win is spoken of as oue of the fnest speakers of, and

preachers in, Itish in Munster. Now, when arrange-
By ments have been made on the part of .the authorities of
the Cabhgdral of Cork for an Irish sermon. on St. Pat-
tick’s. it. is most desirable that as many as possible of
| those who can follow an Irish sermon should attend. It
would, in the natural order, be disheartening not only
to the preacher, but to those who had taken the trouble
to secure the services of a distinguished Irish preacher if
a very large proportion of the congregation was not com-
1 posed of Trish speakers. We trust that all our Catholic
Irish understanding readers will make it their business
o be at the Cathedral, Cork, on St. Patrick’s Day.

Last week we stated that we were informed that the
authorities of the Good Shepherd Convent, Waterford,
bought their soap for laundry work exclusively from
English manufacturers. We used the information, as
the source of it was, in our opinion, very reliable. We
asked, “Is this an oversight or what?”" Since our last
week’s issue we have had evidence laid before us that
the word “exclusively” overstated the case, for we have
seen invoices of soap supplied by an Irish soap manu-
facturer to the Convent in 1903 and 1904. The Con-
vent also, we learn, buys soap from a local distributing
firm, but about the nationality of that soap we know
nothing. A great deal of soap used in the laundry is
procured from England, but it is claimed that it is
cheaper and better. Soap is a big item in a great
laundry, and what we may call the finished product of
a laundry, the washing, must be done for the public at
competitive prices. We are swre that the Convent

would give the preference, subject to price and value
being all right, to Irish manufacturers. = Whether the
Convent has gone out of its way to find an Irish soap
that competes with. the portion of the soap it imports we

do not know; but the Irish soap manufacturers should .

now look after this item, which must be a large one in
the year, and show, as we are confident they can, that
they can supply as good if not a better article at the
same price as the English firm. We are sure that the

authorities of the Convent will be only too pleased to.

support Irish manufacture. ~ We hope shortly to hear
thab one or more Irish soap manufacturers have been able
to wash all traces of Saxon soap from the Good Shepherd
Convent, of Waterford.

©f Irish manufacture—a movement to the resuscitation

allude to, in the Rotunda and its gardems, Rutland
Square .

T visited every stall of that exhibition within and
without, and made, during many days, extensive notes.
Hayving put my notes, remarks, and observations into
strict order, I resolved Lo probe the question so as to see
how far the exhilits were a mere glitter and clever
decoy, and how far an honest, truthful parade of
articles exhibited in the country in which they were
supposed to have been made. ith this view I went

were answered in a fair honourable style, I was con-

as wool was concerned, I knew a good deal about it as
I had spent the best years of my life in South Africa

an -Angora from that of a common sheep.. Moreover,
I had heen over all Yorkshire, including Bradford,
Massingham and Dewsbury, and had kept a well regu-

well posted. However, after leaving the factories, I
visited about twenty-five good-sized general shops, a_nd
was swrprised and saddened to find no end of English
ware of every kind put pretty well into the front row
for sale in the heart of Belfast—a city remarkable for
real Irish trade. I conversed with, say 100 persons,
enthusiastically about the folly and the shame of
patronising foreign goods so long as we could get in
Ireland goods of Irish manufacture—and will you be-

I shall not trouble you by reciting my visits to other
cities of Ireland, but will now come to Dublin itself
where the exhibition, inaugurated by honest, patriotic

sad spectacle—interlaced and interwoven, and inter-
twined as I found it in every department of trade by

facture seemed to smother the few real Irish articles
that lay under dust on musty shelves.

As T had previously studied the history of Irish in-
dustries for a hundred years before the Dargan Exhi-
bition, and from the Dargan Exhibition forward, I was
able to enter upon my self-imposed work with a light
heart.

I began at the Sanctuary by asking: How many
Churches, Convents, Schools and Seminaries have been
built since 1829? Have the materials of all these eccle-

Then I enquired: What about the Sacred Vestments
and Altar linens, and chalices, and ciboriums and
Pyxes, and Holy Yil Stocks, and Monstrances? * Are
they of Irish Manufacture? Could they be? They are all
sold in Ireland and notably in Dublin, but are they

manufactured—partly or wholly in Ireland? If uot,
why not? Whose fault is it? I learned that the
public and private ecclesiastical outlay.or outpay in
Ireland would keep many thousands in constant em-
ployment.

At Smyth’s silver establishment in Wicklow Street,

patronage, he could keep 500 hands instead of ten.

The examination of this question was on delicate
ground, and the result was enough to make angels
weep over poor Catholic Iveland sending away the
steady strain of money collections to other countries,

THE CORK PLAYS.
ORK is a place where you always fecl you ’are
among Irishmen. And when you visit it, you
e, the lesson so often borne in upon the Dubliner
that Lo be prosperous you must needs be English. But
if Cork people are Irish, it is by no means always in the
Irish Treland sense of the word. I suppose there are
mere boys sent from Cork to English schools than from
any other part of Ireland, and all that they may seek
to unlearn the most musical accent in the country.
Again, on a recent, visit after reading with pleasure the
rish names over the shops, I was much disgu
from casual conversation that “ A Chinese H
—a_peculiarly objectionable s
garity, was the principal topic of public intere
time.

It was then pleasant to visit a Cork dramatic
formance in which the items were Irish both in subject
and in authorship, and not only Irish but 6 part new
and original productions. Original production, literary
self-criticism is the great intellectual need of Ireland
and the Cork National Theatre Society is to be thanked
for adding to our scanty stock. The plays on this occa-
sion were three in number—* The Last the Des-
monds,” by Miss Milligan, Yeats's “ Kathleen ni Houli-
han,” and the “Nation Builders,” by a dramatist
Mr. John O’Loughlan, of Cork. They were interesting
as presenting a historical sequence. “The Last of the
Desmonds™ was, of course, in the time of Elizabeth, and
dealt with the last flickers of Irish independence.

*“ Kathleen ni Houlihan” is placed in the fateful year
1798. “The Nation Builders” takes place in our own
day and tells of the work of the Irish Revival.

The honowrs of the- first piece were generally
admitted to have been borne off by Miss Gould-
ing, who acted the part of the old nurse Marie
Sheehy. I don’t know that costume plays, es-
pecially temp. Eliz., are the best ones for amateur
sacieties. It is so hard to seem Elizabethan, without
practice, and you have always to beware of Shakspere
as a-rival. The play dealt with young James Fitzgerald,
the Desmond diglon, who, educated in England, has
emerged from the Tudor nest an ugly duckling, coming
back to Ireland as a Protestant loyalist.

I have seen Kathleen ni Houlihan three times, the
first time when it was brought out in the far from
stately swrroundinys in Clarendon Street, on which occa-
sion Mrs. Gonne M‘Bride acted the part of Kathleen.
Each time that I have seen it my appreciation has
increased. I think that, except the “Heather Field,”
and the “Bending of the Bough,” it is the best thing
owr Irish dramatists have done. A strong political in-
terest for once brought Mr. Yeates down from the
clouds and made him speak in a symbolism that is
intelligible. The idea of the young man leaving every-
thing to follow Ireland under the guise of the Sean
Vean Voct on the day of Castlebar, is one that has
much more reality in it than a-nybhing that Mr. Yeates
has since put forth. I am rather inclined to agree with
a_writer who recently suggested that in the Abbey
Theatre, the habitation has been improved and the in-
mates have deteriorated. On this occasion the acting
was creditable, but not as good as I have seen it. Per-
haps it was that in having Mrs. Gonne-M‘Bride for the
part of Kathleen, there was a touch of realism that
must necessarily be absent from any other impersona-
tion. The father and mother were well done, but the
son was a little too substantial to suit the character of
the visionary enthusiast.

“The Nation Builders” was a most original play. Evi-
dently propagandist in its character, it was intended as

dustrial side. It dealt willy the fortunes not of a man
but-of a company, and perhaps at the present day we
have more need to consider,the life-stories of directors
than ‘of heroes. I have said the interest was not a
personal one, but the author, so far consented to hearken
to convention as to interweave a lovestory, and
the Gne]lc League hero, Dr. FitzGerald, had the task of
converting and winning a charming heroine in the per-
son of Sheila. Murphy, the manufacturer’s daughter.
The moral of the piece is that Irish industry should
depend on Irish capital, and in truth we hear a greal
deal too, about attracting English investors who'seldom
come, and if they even do, come for no useful purpose.
John Murphy. a local manufacturer of West-British
ideas, decides to turn his business into a company to be
floated with English capital, and calls in a Leeds Com-
pany promoter to assist him. The result is ruin, from
which he is only saved by the timely intervention of
Dr. Fitzgerald and a local committce who save the
works from being shut up, and appoint him manager.
Mr. Murphy's West British son, expert in naught save
hockey, was well drawn, but I fear Mr. W. Finn was
too good an Ivishman to assume the haw-haw style with
perfect success. They should have hired or borrowed a
West Briton. The great success of the play, and what
I think will recommend it to other societies, was the
creditors’ meeting, a scene worthy of the “Bending
of the Bough.” Mr. T. Murphy’s acting in this scene
as Denis Twomey, R.D.C. and champion “ramaisher,”
who frequently throughout proclaims his belief
in  “technical instruction” as a remedy for all
human ills, was magnificent. I do not remember to
have seen a creditors’ meeting on the stage before, and
yet if anyone has ever been at one he will realise that
it is a scene eminently suited for dramatic treatment.
The tone of the play was strongly Irish, so s
that the * Zzaminer” practically ran away from cri
ing it. The author lost no opportunity of preaching the
doctrines ot Irish Ireland. It might, indeed, be sug-
gested as a fair criticism on the play, as is often said in
regard to Dickens's propagandist novels, that it would
be better if the reader or listener were left to infer from
the action some of the doctrines enunciated by the
characters. The latter way is, however, very much the
tendency of the French dramatists. The play was
very well received, especially the scene I have referred
to, and the large hall was filled with a crowded audience.
I hope that the Cork National Theatre Society may
thrive and produce other original plays at succeeding

HE opinion seems to be growing that Technical
T Education in Ireland is likely to be a failurve.
Its most ardent supporters seem to be getting disgusted:
with it; and in many parts of the country its life is in
rather serious danger; yet we need it now as much as we
did a year or two ago, when we went into raptures over
it; indeed, every day that passes makes our need of it
the greater. Would it not be a wiser and more logical
course then, if, instead of passing resolutions, to wipe it
out of the country (we are so fond of sweeping measures);,
the resolution-makers set to work to find out what is the
reason that Technical Education that has done so much
for other countries is doing so little for us. This would,
no doubt, be easier, if the Department, instead of snub-
bing Technical Committees and referring them to “ Ap-
pendix C,” listened to their sometimes useful sugges-
tions.  Dignity is all very well, but too much of it
seldom makes things hum, to use a LEADER phrase, and
with all due respect to the Department; many of their
schemes and their methods of imparting knowledge need
reforming. The resolution of the Limerick Techuical
Committee could not, perhaps, be truthfully echoed all

over the country; but if itinerant instruction has not

been exactly barren of result everywhere, it has cer-
tainly not worked wonders. Take for example the Do-

mestic Economy Lectures, we all know the opinion of

The present arrangement for teaching this very useful
science, is generally a six-week’s course of lectures on
Cookery and Laundry. Of cowse this is very good as
far as it goes, but it does not go far enough. ~ It would
never make a nation of musicians, to teach every child in
the country the scale, and cram a classical piece or
twa into them directly, with the badly learned scale as
sole foundation, and then leave them to themselves.
Neither will a six week’s course of cookery lectures,
aliways the same, in five or six districts in each county
vearly, ever make us a nation of good cooks and thrifty
housewifes.  The Department may be quite sure of
that. It might, to be sure, in time do some little good,
but we are living in an age that expects and must have
quick returns. And in nothing is it more important
than in this matter, that some fruit should be produced
quickly; because the Irish people have not only to be
educated, but their prejudice against Technical Educa-
tion has to be overcome. * And, therefore, it will not
do to postpone to some happy future time, all the good

~resulting from it, a little of it is wanted quickly as a
sort of pledge of the greater things that are to follow.

I would suggest then, instead of trying to teach girls,
who probably cannot wash a saucepan properly, to do
all kinds of plain and even highclass cooking in six
weeks, with the result, of course, of teaching them very
little of anything, the following way, which also, I am
sure, could be much improved upon. In every district,
instead of the six-week’s course, have one of three
months. Let the first half of this time be devoted to the
thorough: learning of the very simplest things, but above
all of cleanliness; and the other half to all plain kinds
of cookery, and household management. But no girl
De allowed to attend this more advanced course, who had
not thoroughly mastered the first and simpler one. And
finally, that the three best girls out of the whole class
be selected and sent for a further six-weeks’ course in
a Domestic Economy Training School ; these schools to
be attached to the model farms, which the department
are going to have in each county. Here the girls could
learn to apply practically, what they had been taught
before ; the actual housekeeping and managing for the
whole Institution could be done by their help, and if
they came from rural districts, they could also get lessons
on butter-making and poultry management, besides hav-
ing the advantage of seeing something of the working of
a really up-to-date farm.

To some people, this may seem an altogether too ela-
borate way of teaching such a simple subject; I would
remind them, that at best, it is difficult to learn Domes-
tic Edonomy from lectures, and that it is only mere
foolishness to imagine that it can be taught in six weels,
of five two-hour days. Any lady who has ever tried to
train an inexperienced servant, can anderstand how
little can be done, in such a short time. And then as
these lectures are always exactly the same, when they
are given secondly in a district, scarcely one of the
former pupils ever attend. And surely, when there are
so' many scholarships for boys, it would be only fair to

. _ let the girls have some too.

Of course, under favourable circumstances, much
might be done by the present system, but it must be
remembered, favourable circumstances do not exist; and

that the present system is not giving satisfaction; is no

Around the farmer’s fire, when an instructress isin
the district, can often be heard plenty of sarcastic ailu-
sions to her work, but very little praise. One person
will say, “Our Maggie had home yesterday a bit of some
black thing boiled in a cup, much good it uld be to a
ploughman;” or, “Our Mary has me bothered to buy

we always med the pancakes on, and as good

that one.” Did you ever hear the like, throwin’ them
up in the air to turn them. Faith, only for the priest
talkin’ so much about the same lectures, I'd never let
her back. What is the world comin’ to?’ she winds
up. “And what is the world comin’ to?” echoes her lis-
teners, and so they dismiss the subject, and begin to
discuss somebody’s new bonnet, or the advisability of
giving cayenne pepper to a few picked hens to make
them lay in the winter; incidentally remarking with
more or less vehemence, that there was nothing like
the ’ould breeds. And go the efforts of poor Maggie and
Mary at advancement, meeting with nothing but ridi-
cule, and the new ideas not having an opportunity of
getting rooted deeply enough, die a natural death. And
some time after, somebody comes home from America,
and tells highly-coloured stories of life over there, and
Maggie and Mary listen, and being, naturally, refined
girls, fond of pleasant, beautiful things, they begin to
hate their ugly surroundings, their coarse untidy meals
(which really might not be so) the eternal cooking for
the pigs; and so one day they leave it all behind, and
Ireland as well, and start for the wonderful “beyant.”

Now, I do not mean to say that by a more extended
course of lectures and scholarships, this attitude of the
people and all its altendant consequences, would be
entirely removed. But the Irish are a competitive race,
though they may be last on the list for so far, and that
they are proud, goes without saying. These Scholarships.
would be tangible things to work for, and assuredly,
many girls would be induced to attend who would not
otherwise do so; and more important still, the competi-
tive spirit would be upon them, and they would do their
very best to profit by the instruction given. Then it
would ensure, at least, nine thoroughly trained house-
keepers in every county yearly, not to mention the large
numbers of fairly well-trained ones; and these would
naturally influence many others. These girls, it is to be
hoped, would not only have learned tv make a cake, ov
cook an eatable dinner, but they would also have learned
so well the lessons of tidiness and thrift and the com-
forts resulting from them, that they would be able to
overcome any amount of criticism and ridicule in their
efforts to improve their homes.

Then, too, these Scholarships, would bel found to in—
crease the popularity, of all kinds of techuical teaching.
The small farmer who is very often an inveterate enemy

tainly look upon il with softened feelings; when his.
daughter would head the list at the cookery class, and so-
with many of its other opponents.

And that Irish women need this training, anyone who
knows Ireland at all knows. In England there is much
talk about the physical degeneration of the race; in
Ireland we have the degeneration of so many things to
talk of, we hear very little about this. Yet there is very:
marked difference, say, between the agricultural popula~
tion of to-day and of forty years ago. They are better
paid, belter housed, and generally their condition has
improved, yet they are not nearly asfine men physically,

Since writing the above, I have read in M. O'R.’s
article in THE LEADER. 25th February, of the method
adopted in Fermanagh, of teaching Domestic Economy.
Tt is certainly the very best way, and could scarcely be
improved upon; but I am afraid it would be only suit-
able in a very few places. I can imagine how the
average Irish farmer’s wife would look if she heard that
her kitchen and house were going to be invaded by a
teacher. There is a certain great German industry
which we sometimes hear about from lecturers on Tech-
nical Education, the owner of it has ideas about ventila-
tion and drainage, and things of that sort, so she in-
troduces all the new improvements of these into the
cottages of some of her workmen. It is no doubt for
their ultimate benefit, but instead of being grateful, the
workmen very often resent her experimenting as an un-
warrantable intexference with their liberty. I am afraid
the wives of Irish farmers would resent just as bitterly,
ladies going in to their houses to teach them, in fact
the greater portion of them, would never consent to it.
I remember a few years ago, somebody got a leacher
down from Glasnevin to this part of the country, to give
a few lectures on Butter-making, and visit the farmers’
dairies and suggest improvements. The lectures went
off all right, there was a big attendance, which, perhaps,
was to be accounted for by the fact that lectures were
‘more of a novelty then than now; but the visiting!—
a locked door met the lecturer in quite a large number
of places, or, indeed, in every place where she was most

ToE CARMELITE CHURCH, WHITEFRIARS' ST.

Ou' Sunday next a meeting will be held in the Carmelite
Church,” Whitefriars’ St., Dublin, immediately after
12 o'clock Mass, at which his Grace the Archbishop of
Dublin. will preside and the Lord Mayor will attend.
The object needs no commendation, being to organise
a fund towards the cost of the recent necessary renova-
tion which the Carmelite buildings have undergone. The
interior has been entirely repainted and otherwise
decorated, and an electrical installation has been in-
troduced. A fine organ now in course of construction,
will shortly be added to these improvements. It should
be stated that the large amount of work involved has
been given to Irish firms to execute. Messrs. Martin
Son and Co., Stephen’s Green, did the painting and
decorative part. Messrs. Cummins and Sons, Lower
Abbey Street, performed the electrical portion, and
Messrs. J. and H. White, York Street, are building the
organ. Sunday’s meeting should result in the success
it deserves. Though some people would build for them-
selves a house of cedarwood, and a hut of skins for the
House of God; the mere Trish still cling to a higher
ideal which the growth of the “economic sense” among
them any more than the trials of centuries caunot
destroy.
